PhD candidate in Technology and Innovation Management at the University of Pretoria, researching mass timber adoption in South African construction.

I am a PhD candidate at the University of Pretoria's Graduate School of Technology Management (GSTM), where my research focuses on why the South African construction industry has been slow to adopt mass timber. The theoretical backbone is UTAUT (Unified Theory of Acceptance and Use of Technology), borrowed from the Information Systems literature and applied to a construction context. Beyond the PhD itself, I have a broader interest in adoption theories, lifecycle assessment, and sustainable construction materials generally.

Before Pretoria, I completed a BSc in Earth Science (First Class Honours) at the University of Ghana and an MSc in Project Management (with Distinction) at the University of Pretoria. The path between those two degrees included four years of teaching at a senior high school in Ghana's Volta Region, volunteer work with NSBE Ghana, a stint as a content editor, and a role coordinating coaching and consultancy projects at Cole Africa. All of that shaped how I think about research, communication, and working with limited resources.

At GSTM I wear several hats: I develop the module content for ETM700 (Technology and Innovation Management) at postgraduate level, supervise Honours research studies, co-supervise Masters students, and deliver training on qualitative research methods and ATLAS.ti. I also contribute to broader efforts promoting timber construction in South Africa through research and publishing. My [ResearchGate profile](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Ferdinand-Hassan) has a current list of publications.

Consulting on postgraduate theses and dissertations, from structural reviews to feedback incorporation.

I do consulting work for postgraduate students who need help with their theses and dissertations. The work is substantive: interpreting supervisor or examiner feedback, restructuring arguments, reworking theoretical framing, or helping a student get a chapter from a rough draft to something defensible. I also help with methodology, particularly survey design, systematic literature reviews, and qualitative research.

The consulting comes out of my own research experience (published papers, a PhD nearing completion, co-supervision of several students through to publication) and a genuine interest in helping people produce work they can be proud of.

Helping Ghanaian small and medium enterprises through the tender and procurement process.

I am starting a project with a friend to help small and medium enterprises in Ghana through the tender and procurement process. The idea grew out of watching SMEs with good capacity lose out on contracts because they did not know how to put a bid together or meet compliance requirements. We are still in the early stages, and more details will follow as the project takes shape.

PhD: Mass Timber Adoption in South African Construction
Why has the South African construction industry been slow to adopt mass timber? A PhD by publication using UTAUT.

My PhD asks why the South African construction industry has been slow to adopt mass timber. The theoretical backbone is UTAUT (Unified Theory of Acceptance and Use of Technology), borrowed from the Information Systems literature and respecified for construction material adoption.

## Structure

The PhD is structured as a PhD by publication: an introduction chapter, seven papers, and a conclusion forming a single coherent narrative. Papers 1 through 3 are systematic literature reviews covering UTAUT in construction, mass timber adoption factors, and technology adoption in the South African construction industry. Paper 4 integrates these into a conceptual framework. Paper 5 tests the model quantitatively via PLS-SEM. Papers 6 and 7 are the qualitative phase: sequential explanatory interviews with 23 South African construction professionals (Paper 6) and a UTAUT respecification for construction material adoption (Paper 7).

## Methods

The study follows a sequential explanatory mixed-methods design. The quantitative phase tested the conceptual model via partial least squares structural equation modelling, and the qualitative phase draws on semi-structured interviews analysed through thematic analysis.

## Status

Papers 1 through 5 are complete and published. Data collection for Papers 6 and 7 is complete, with all themes saturated across 23 participants. Both papers are in the drafting phase. The introduction chapter has a full strategy but no sections written yet.

Co-supervision of Masters and Honours students on adoption, risk management, and innovation studies.

I co-supervise Masters and Honours students at the University of Pretoria, typically on studies related to adoption, risk management, and innovation in the built environment and industrial engineering. Topics so far have ranged from open innovation adoption in timber construction to project risk management in South African construction, enterprise risk management at PRASA, and disruptive innovation in start-ups. Several of these projects have produced peer-reviewed publications in *SAJIE* and at the ASOCSA Built Environment Conference.

Timber Construction Feasibility in South Africa
Can structural timber work in South Africa? Research on building standards, feasibility, and industry perceptions.

This line of work sits alongside the PhD and asks a practical question: can structural timber actually work in South Africa? The research examines building standards compliance (strength, fire, moisture resistance), comparative feasibility across different contexts, the economic case for mass timber buildings, and how industry professionals perceive timber's quality relative to conventional materials.

So far, this has produced a journal article in *Wood Material Science & Engineering* on structural timber feasibility, a paper at WCTE 2025 in Brisbane comparing feasibility perspectives, a conference paper at ASOCSA 2024 on perceived quality and barriers, and a paper presented in Vilnius on the economic feasibility of mass timber buildings.

Subject matter expert and content developer for the ETM700 postgraduate module.

ETM700 is a postgraduate module at NQF Level 8 covering technology and innovation management, delivered to postgraduate diploma and honours students at the GSTM. I develop the module content: weekly lectures, prescribed and recommended readings, application exercises tied to a recurring case company, and assessments. The work involves translating current research and real industry cases into material that postgraduate students can actually use, which is a different challenge from writing for a journal audience.

Supervision of honours research studies at the GSTM.

I supervise honours students doing their research studies in IGB/ISC 780 at the GSTM. In practice this means guiding students from topic selection through to a completed research report: helping them frame a workable research question, structure a literature review, choose and apply a methodology, and write up their findings. Most of the students I supervise work on topics related to adoption, innovation, or risk management in the built environment and industrial engineering.

Regular training on qualitative methods and ATLAS.ti for Masters students at the GSTM.

I run training sessions on qualitative research methods and ATLAS.ti for Masters students at the GSTM. What started as a one-off has become a regular departmental request. The sessions cover research design for interview-based studies, thematic analysis, and hands-on use of ATLAS.ti for coding and data management. I draw heavily on my own PhD experience with semi-structured interviews and ATLAS.ti, which makes the training grounded in actual research practice rather than software tutorials.

Co-supervision of Masters students on adoption, risk management, and innovation topics.

I co-supervise Masters students at the GSTM, typically on topics related to adoption, risk management, and innovation in the built environment or industrial engineering. My role sits alongside the primary supervisor, and I tend to get involved most heavily on methodology (survey design, systematic literature reviews, qualitative research design) and on the writing itself. Several of these students have gone on to co-author peer-reviewed publications, which is one of the more satisfying parts of the work.
